title: Tuberous Sclerosis
oneLiner: Autosomal dominant multisystem disorder of benign tumours (hamartomas) in brain, skin, kidney, heart, lungs; presents with epilepsy, developmental delay, and skin lesions.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Status epilepticus:** Prolonged seizure >5 minutes – look for ongoing convulsions or subtle motor activity with impaired consciousness. Immediate management per local protocol (buccal midazolam, IV lorazepam, then phenytoin/phenobarbital). Do not assume it’s “just another fit” – delay can lead to irreversible brain injury.
- **Acute haemorrhage from renal angiomyolipoma:** Sudden flank pain + hypotension + falling Hb – the single finding is *rapidly enlarging flank mass* with shock. Requires immediate CT abdomen, crossmatch, IV fluids, and urgent urology/IR referral. Do not miss this in a known TSC patient with abdominal pain.
- **Tension pneumothorax in lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM):** Respiratory distress + absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation – needle decompression before chest X-ray if life-threatening. Most common in young women with TSC; a spontaneous pneumothorax should raise suspicion for LAM.
- **Hydrocephalus from SEGA (subependymal giant cell astrocytoma):** Headache + vomiting + reduced GCS ± papilloedema – urgent CT head, neurosurgery referral. Do not attribute these symptoms to migraine or viral illness in a TSC patient.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Seizures (infantile spasms, focal, generalised; often refractory)
  - Developmental delay / regression (especially language and motor)
  - Skin lesions (hypomelanotic macules, facial angiofibromas, shagreen patch, periungual fibromas)
  - Renal angiomyolipomas (may be asymptomatic or cause flank pain, haematuria)
  - Respiratory (dyspnoea, recurrent pneumothorax in LAM)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Wood’s lamp:** hypopigmented macules (“ash-leaf spots”) – often first sign in infants
  - **Face:** angiofibromas (butterfly distribution, pink/yellow papules)
  - **Trunk:** shagreen patch (lumbosacral, flesh-coloured, cobblestone)
  - **Nails:** periungual fibromas (Koenen tumours)
  - **Eyes:** retinal hamartomas (ophthalmoscopy: mulberry-like or flat)
  - **Heart:** murmur (rhabdomyoma – often resolves spontaneously)
  - **Abdomen:** palpable mass (large angiomyolipoma or polycystic kidneys)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1):** Café-au-lait spots vs hypomelanotic macules; NF1 has axillary freckling, Lisch nodules, neurofibromas. **Splitter:** hypomelanotic macules + shagreen patch = TSC, not NF1.
- **Sturge-Weber syndrome:** Port-wine stain (facial, V1 distribution), ipsilateral leptomeningeal angioma, glaucoma – no hypomelanotic macules.
- **Focal cortical dysplasia (isolated epilepsy):** No skin lesions, no renal/LAM features; MRI brain shows cortical tuber-like lesions but no other TSC hallmarks.
- **Renal cell carcinoma:** Isolated renal mass without skin/neurological features; in TSC, risk of RCC is increased but angiomyolipoma is more common and has characteristic fat on imaging.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - **Wood’s lamp** (all new seizure patients/developmental delay)
  - Blood glucose, ECG (if arrhythmia or cardiac murmur)
  - Urine dip (haematuria in renal AML)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, coagulation (if haemorrhage suspected)
  - Calcium, glucose (seizure workup)
  - Consider genetic testing (TSC1/TSC2) for confirmation
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Renal ultrasound (angiomyolipomas, cysts); brain MRI (tubers, SEGA, subependymal nodules)
  - **Second-line:** CT chest (LAM; typical cysts); CT abdomen (if acute haemorrhage or complex AML); echocardiogram (rhabdomyoma – usually in infants)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** 2012 International TSC Consensus Criteria
  - **Major features (≥2 for definite):** Hypomelanotic macules (≥3, ≥5mm), angiofibromas (≥3)/fibrous cephalic plaque, ungual fibromas (≥2), shagreen patch, multiple retinal hamartomas, cortical dysplasias, subependymal nodules, SEGA, cardiac rhabdomyoma, LAM, angiomyolipomas (≥2)
  - **Minor features (1 major + ≥2 minor = definite):** “Confetti” skin lesions, dental enamel pits, intraoral fibromas, retinal achromic patch, multiple renal cysts, nonrenal hamartomas
- **Never-miss test:** **Wood’s lamp examination** in any young patient with new-onset seizures or developmental delay – a missed hypomelanotic macule delays diagnosis and risk of preventable complications (e.g., SEGA surveillance)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Status epilepticus:** Treat per local protocol – usually buccal midazolam, then IV lorazepam; escalate to phenytoin/phenobarbital; call neurology
  - **Suspected renal haemorrhage:** IV access, crossmatch, fluid resuscitation; urgent CT; urology/interventional radiology consult for possible embolisation
  - **Tension pneumothorax:** Immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS midclavicular line) followed by chest drain
  - **Hydrocephalus from SEGA:** Urgent CT head, neurosurgery referral for CSF diversion or SEGA resection
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Seizure control:** Start antiepileptic – levetiracetam (250-500mg BD, titrate; use BNF) or carbamazepine/valproate; for infantile spasms, consider vigabatrin [check local policy]
  - **SEGA surveillance:** MRI brain (if not done); if growing/symptomatic, start everolimus [check local policy dose]
  - **Renal:** If angiomyolipoma >4cm, discuss mTOR inhibitor (everolimus) or prophylactic embolisation with urology
  - **LAM:** If symptomatic, consider sirolimus/everolimus; pulmonary rehab; oxygen if hypoxic
  - **Cardiac echocardiogram** if rhabdomyoma suspected (usually in infants; many regress)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Genetic counselling (TSC1/TSC2)
  - Annual renal ultrasound (angiomyolipoma growth)
  - Brain MRI every 1-3 years (SEGA surveillance)
  - Pulmonary function tests (if LAM)
  - Dermatology for skin lesions (laser for angiofibromas if symptomatic)
  - Neurodevelopmental team (autism, ADHD, learning disability)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** Maintenance IV fluids (e.g., 0.9% NaCl + 5% glucose) if NBM or poor oral intake; if haemorrhage, fluid resuscitate per local protocol
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin only if no bleeding risk (e.g., after haemorrhage) – discuss with senior; consider mechanical prophylaxis (TED stockings)
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM if likely for MRI or surgery
  - **Observations:** Neurological observations (GCS, pupils) 4 hourly post-seizure; NEWS2 4 hourly (more frequent if unstable)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Status epilepticus:** Buccal midazolam 10mg (adult); IV lorazepam 4mg; if refractory, phenytoin 15-18mg/kg IV [check local policy]
  - **Chronic epilepsy:** Levetiracetam 250-500mg BD, titrate to response; carbamazepine or valproate as alternatives
  - **Infantile spasms (TSC-specific):** Vigabatrin – starting dose 50mg/kg/day, titrate [check local policy due to visual field risk]
  - **SEGA/renal AML:** Everolimus – starting dose 2.5-5mg daily [check local policy]; adjust for hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B)
  - **LAM:** Sirolimus or everolimus [check local policy]
- **Common alternatives:** Topiramate, lamotrigine (for epilepsy); mTOR inhibitors are mainstay for SEGA/AML/LAM
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - mTOR inhibitors with strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin, phenytoin) – reduce dose of mTOR; with inhibitors (azole antifungals, macrolides) – increase risk of toxicity
  - Vigabatrin – contraindicated in pre-existing visual field defects; monitor for peripheral vision loss
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Everolimus – dose reduction for moderate hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B); avoid if severe (Child-Pugh C). Levetiracetam – dose adjustment in CKD (creatinine clearance <50 mL/min)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Everolimus overdose** can cause severe mucositis, infections, hyperglycemia – always check dose adjustments for hepatic/renal impairment and avoid simultaneous strong CYP3A4 inducers/inhibitors without discussing with pharmacy
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Status epilepticus
  - Renal angiomyolipoma haemorrhage (life-threatening, can mimic acute abdomen)
  - Spontaneous pneumothorax (LAM)
  - Hydrocephalus (SEGA)
  - Cardiac arrhythmia / outflow obstruction (rhabdomyoma, usually neonatal)
- **Chronic:**
  - Intellectual disability, autism, ADHD
  - Refractory epilepsy
  - Renal failure (polycystic kidney disease, repeated AML haemorrhage, renal cell carcinoma)
  - Progressive LAM (dyspnoea, hypoxemia, respiratory failure)
  - SEGA growth (hydrocephalus, raised ICP)
  - Skin disfigurement (angiofibromas)
sections.Prognosis: - Variable spectrum – many live into adulthood with significant epilepsy, learning disability, and renal/respiratory morbidity. Leading causes of death: renal disease (haemorrhage, RCC), SEGA-related hydrocephalus, LAM (respiratory failure), and cardiac rhabdomyoma in infancy. With mTOR inhibitors, survival and quality of life have improved for SEGA and AML.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Use Wood’s lamp** on all infants presenting with first seizure or developmental regression – hypomelanotic macules are pathognomonic and often missed in normal light.
- **In a young woman with spontaneous pneumothorax**, ask about skin lesions and family history of TSC; consider CT chest for LAM and renal ultrasound.
- **If a known TSC patient presents with acute abdominal/flank pain**, always suspect renal angiomyolipoma haemorrhage – request urgent CT with contrast (if stable) and crossmatch.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not attribute seizures to TSC without ruling out other causes** (infection, electrolyte disturbance, subdural haematoma) – always check temperature, glucose, and consider imaging if atypical.
- **Do not ignore growth of SEGA** on routine MRI – failing to start everolimus or refer neurosurgery can lead to avoidable hydrocephalus and brain damage.
- **Avoid using NSAIDs** in patients with known renal angiomyolipomas – increased bleeding risk; instead use paracetamol for pain.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Seizure lasting >5 minutes or more than one seizure in 24 hours
  - Sudden severe headache, vomiting, or blurred vision (hydrocephalus from SEGA)
  - Sudden flank pain, haematuria, or sync

rawMarkdown:
## Tuberous Sclerosis
**One-liner:** Autosomal dominant multisystem disorder of benign tumours (hamartomas) in brain, skin, kidney, heart, lungs; presents with epilepsy, developmental delay, and skin lesions.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Status epilepticus:** Prolonged seizure >5 minutes – look for ongoing convulsions or subtle motor activity with impaired consciousness. Immediate management per local protocol (buccal midazolam, IV lorazepam, then phenytoin/phenobarbital). Do not assume it’s “just another fit” – delay can lead to irreversible brain injury.
- **Acute haemorrhage from renal angiomyolipoma:** Sudden flank pain + hypotension + falling Hb – the single finding is *rapidly enlarging flank mass* with shock. Requires immediate CT abdomen, crossmatch, IV fluids, and urgent urology/IR referral. Do not miss this in a known TSC patient with abdominal pain.
- **Tension pneumothorax in lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM):** Respiratory distress + absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation – needle decompression before chest X-ray if life-threatening. Most common in young women with TSC; a spontaneous pneumothorax should raise suspicion for LAM.
- **Hydrocephalus from SEGA (subependymal giant cell astrocytoma):** Headache + vomiting + reduced GCS ± papilloedema – urgent CT head, neurosurgery referral. Do not attribute these symptoms to migraine or viral illness in a TSC patient.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Seizures (infantile spasms, focal, generalised; often refractory)
  - Developmental delay / regression (especially language and motor)
  - Skin lesions (hypomelanotic macules, facial angiofibromas, shagreen patch, periungual fibromas)
  - Renal angiomyolipomas (may be asymptomatic or cause flank pain, haematuria)
  - Respiratory (dyspnoea, recurrent pneumothorax in LAM)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Wood’s lamp:** hypopigmented macules (“ash-leaf spots”) – often first sign in infants
  - **Face:** angiofibromas (butterfly distribution, pink/yellow papules)
  - **Trunk:** shagreen patch (lumbosacral, flesh-coloured, cobblestone)
  - **Nails:** periungual fibromas (Koenen tumours)
  - **Eyes:** retinal hamartomas (ophthalmoscopy: mulberry-like or flat)
  - **Heart:** murmur (rhabdomyoma – often resolves spontaneously)
  - **Abdomen:** palpable mass (large angiomyolipoma or polycystic kidneys)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1):** Café-au-lait spots vs hypomelanotic macules; NF1 has axillary freckling, Lisch nodules, neurofibromas. **Splitter:** hypomelanotic macules + shagreen patch = TSC, not NF1.
- **Sturge-Weber syndrome:** Port-wine stain (facial, V1 distribution), ipsilateral leptomeningeal angioma, glaucoma – no hypomelanotic macules.
- **Focal cortical dysplasia (isolated epilepsy):** No skin lesions, no renal/LAM features; MRI brain shows cortical tuber-like lesions but no other TSC hallmarks.
- **Renal cell carcinoma:** Isolated renal mass without skin/neurological features; in TSC, risk of RCC is increased but angiomyolipoma is more common and has characteristic fat on imaging.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - **Wood’s lamp** (all new seizure patients/developmental delay)
  - Blood glucose, ECG (if arrhythmia or cardiac murmur)
  - Urine dip (haematuria in renal AML)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, coagulation (if haemorrhage suspected)
  - Calcium, glucose (seizure workup)
  - Consider genetic testing (TSC1/TSC2) for confirmation
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Renal ultrasound (angiomyolipomas, cysts); brain MRI (tubers, SEGA, subependymal nodules)
  - **Second-line:** CT chest (LAM; typical cysts); CT abdomen (if acute haemorrhage or complex AML); echocardiogram (rhabdomyoma – usually in infants)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** 2012 International TSC Consensus Criteria
  - **Major features (≥2 for definite):** Hypomelanotic macules (≥3, ≥5mm), angiofibromas (≥3)/fibrous cephalic plaque, ungual fibromas (≥2), shagreen patch, multiple retinal hamartomas, cortical dysplasias, subependymal nodules, SEGA, cardiac rhabdomyoma, LAM, angiomyolipomas (≥2)
  - **Minor features (1 major + ≥2 minor = definite):** “Confetti” skin lesions, dental enamel pits, intraoral fibromas, retinal achromic patch, multiple renal cysts, nonrenal hamartomas
- **Never-miss test:** **Wood’s lamp examination** in any young patient with new-onset seizures or developmental delay – a missed hypomelanotic macule delays diagnosis and risk of preventable complications (e.g., SEGA surveillance)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Status epilepticus:** Treat per local protocol – usually buccal midazolam, then IV lorazepam; escalate to phenytoin/phenobarbital; call neurology
  - **Suspected renal haemorrhage:** IV access, crossmatch, fluid resuscitation; urgent CT; urology/interventional radiology consult for possible embolisation
  - **Tension pneumothorax:** Immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS midclavicular line) followed by chest drain
  - **Hydrocephalus from SEGA:** Urgent CT head, neurosurgery referral for CSF diversion or SEGA resection
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Seizure control:** Start antiepileptic – levetiracetam (250-500mg BD, titrate; use BNF) or carbamazepine/valproate; for infantile spasms, consider vigabatrin [check local policy]
  - **SEGA surveillance:** MRI brain (if not done); if growing/symptomatic, start everolimus [check local policy dose]
  - **Renal:** If angiomyolipoma >4cm, discuss mTOR inhibitor (everolimus) or prophylactic embolisation with urology
  - **LAM:** If symptomatic, consider sirolimus/everolimus; pulmonary rehab; oxygen if hypoxic
  - **Cardiac echocardiogram** if rhabdomyoma suspected (usually in infants; many regress)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Genetic counselling (TSC1/TSC2)
  - Annual renal ultrasound (angiomyolipoma growth)
  - Brain MRI every 1-3 years (SEGA surveillance)
  - Pulmonary function tests (if LAM)
  - Dermatology for skin lesions (laser for angiofibromas if symptomatic)
  - Neurodevelopmental team (autism, ADHD, learning disability)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** Maintenance IV fluids (e.g., 0.9% NaCl + 5% glucose) if NBM or poor oral intake; if haemorrhage, fluid resuscitate per local protocol
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin only if no bleeding risk (e.g., after haemorrhage) – discuss with senior; consider mechanical prophylaxis (TED stockings)
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM if likely for MRI or surgery
  - **Observations:** Neurological observations (GCS, pupils) 4 hourly post-seizure; NEWS2 4 hourly (more frequent if unstable)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Status epilepticus:** Buccal midazolam 10mg (adult); IV lorazepam 4mg; if refractory, phenytoin 15-18mg/kg IV [check local policy]
  - **Chronic epilepsy:** Levetiracetam 250-500mg BD, titrate to response; carbamazepine or valproate as alternatives
  - **Infantile spasms (TSC-specific):** Vigabatrin – starting dose 50mg/kg/day, titrate [check local policy due to visual field risk]
  - **SEGA/renal AML:** Everolimus – starting dose 2.5-5mg daily [check local policy]; adjust for hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B)
  - **LAM:** Sirolimus or everolimus [check local policy]
- **Common alternatives:** Topiramate, lamotrigine (for epilepsy); mTOR inhibitors are mainstay for SEGA/AML/LAM
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - mTOR inhibitors with strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin, phenytoin) – reduce dose of mTOR; with inhibitors (azole antifungals, macrolides) – increase risk of toxicity
  - Vigabatrin – contraindicated in pre-existing visual field defects; monitor for peripheral vision loss
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Everolimus – dose reduction for moderate hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B); avoid if severe (Child-Pugh C). Levetiracetam – dose adjustment in CKD (creatinine clearance <50 mL/min)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Everolimus overdose** can cause severe mucositis, infections, hyperglycemia – always check dose adjustments for hepatic/renal impairment and avoid simultaneous strong CYP3A4 inducers/inhibitors without discussing with pharmacy

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Status epilepticus
  - Renal angiomyolipoma haemorrhage (life-threatening, can mimic acute abdomen)
  - Spontaneous pneumothorax (LAM)
  - Hydrocephalus (SEGA)
  - Cardiac arrhythmia / outflow obstruction (rhabdomyoma, usually neonatal)
- **Chronic:**
  - Intellectual disability, autism, ADHD
  - Refractory epilepsy
  - Renal failure (polycystic kidney disease, repeated AML haemorrhage, renal cell carcinoma)
  - Progressive LAM (dyspnoea, hypoxemia, respiratory failure)
  - SEGA growth (hydrocephalus, raised ICP)
  - Skin disfigurement (angiofibromas)

### Prognosis
- Variable spectrum – many live into adulthood with significant epilepsy, learning disability, and renal/respiratory morbidity. Leading causes of death: renal disease (haemorrhage, RCC), SEGA-related hydrocephalus, LAM (respiratory failure), and cardiac rhabdomyoma in infancy. With mTOR inhibitors, survival and quality of life have improved for SEGA and AML.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Use Wood’s lamp** on all infants presenting with first seizure or developmental regression – hypomelanotic macules are pathognomonic and often missed in normal light.
- **In a young woman with spontaneous pneumothorax**, ask about skin lesions and family history of TSC; consider CT chest for LAM and renal ultrasound.
- **If a known TSC patient presents with acute abdominal/flank pain**, always suspect renal angiomyolipoma haemorrhage – request urgent CT with contrast (if stable) and crossmatch.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not attribute seizures to TSC without ruling out other causes** (infection, electrolyte disturbance, subdural haematoma) – always check temperature, glucose, and consider imaging if atypical.
- **Do not ignore growth of SEGA** on routine MRI – failing to start everolimus or refer neurosurgery can lead to avoidable hydrocephalus and brain damage.
- **Avoid using NSAIDs** in patients with known renal angiomyolipomas – increased bleeding risk; instead use paracetamol for pain.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Seizure lasting >5 minutes or more than one seizure in 24 hours
  - Sudden severe headache, vomiting, or blurred vision (hydrocephalus from SEGA)
  - Sudden flank pain, haematuria, or sync
